Cloud Summit Vancouver 2026

The Illustrated Primer
to GenAI Networking.
Why we need AI Gateways when we already had API Gateways.

Morgan Foster  ·  Co-Chair, WG AI Gateway 25 min / 22 slides
01  /  Setup

Didn’t we already have API Gateways?

AI Gateways are popping up like weeds. Every vendor has one, every platform team is asking if they need one.
What’s actually different — and why can’t we just use what we have?
The Illustrated Primer to GenAI Networking
02
01  /  Thesis

The body is where the signal is.

Traditional gateways route on headers. AI inference moves every routing, security, and caching decision into the payload.
Traditional API request
# headers carry the decision Host: api.example.com ◄ route Authorization: Bearer ... ◄ auth Content-Type: application/json ◄ negotiate # body is opaque payload { "user_id": 42, "action": "get_balance" }
Everything the gateway needs is in the headers.
AI inference request
# headers are decorative Host: api.openai.com Authorization: Bearer ... # body carries every decision { "model": "gpt-4", ◄ route "messages": [ { "role": "user", ◄ guardrails "content": "..." } ◄ cache key ], "temperature": 0.7 ◄ policy }
Everything the gateway needs is in the body.
Headers vs. Body
03
01  /  The dream deployment

The best-in-class AI gateway.

Every feature on this diagram ships in some product today. Composing them correctly is where it falls apart.
The dream deployment
04
01  /  Transition

Every feature exists.
Composing them is where it breaks.

Problem 1
Data dependencies
Processors consume each other’s output. Order is not a preference.
Problem 2
Retry semantics
Some processors must re-run on failure. Most must not.
Problem 3
Policy scope
Gateway is too broad. Route is too narrow. Destination is missing.
Three problems you didn’t know you had
05
Problem 1 of 3

Data
dependencies.

These aren’t filters. They’re a pipeline.
Processing has data flow between steps
06
Problem 1  /  What the arrows mean

B’s input is A’s output.

  • Cache needs the model. The cache key includes the model — you can’t look up a result until routing resolves.
  • Endpoint picker needs the model. Given a model, pick a replica with warm KV state.
  • Guardrails: the dependency is conditional. Some guardrails need the model (model-specific policies), some don’t (PII, jailbreak). The arrow is dashed — not every guardrail is downstream of routing.
Data dependencies, not just ordering
07
Problem 1  /  What Gateway API gives us today

Filters are ordered. But that’s not enough.

# From the Gateway API spec: # “Wherever possible, implementations # SHOULD implement filters in the order # they are specified.” kind: HTTPRoute spec: rules: - filters: - type: RequestHeaderModifier - type: URLRewrite - type: ExtensionRef # But: no stages. No post-routing # hook. No standard wire format # for external processors.
The spec gives us ordering
Filters run in the order specified — good. But ordering alone doesn’t solve this: credential injection, model-scoped rate limits, and endpoint policy all depend on information that isn’t resolved yet when the request first hits the gateway.
What’s missing
  • Stages. A post_routing stage lets credential injection and endpoint-specific policy run after routing resolves.
  • Pipelines. Explicit data-flow between processors, with per-step retry, timeout, and failure-mode.
  • A wire format. Every vendor rolls their own external-processor protocol. No portability.
 →  stages  +  PayloadProcessingPipeline
Motivates: stages + PayloadProcessingPipeline
08
Problem 2 of 3

Retry
semantics.

When the connection fails, what do you re-run?
Retry is per-processor, not per-request
09
Problem 2  /  Connection fails — the pipeline already ran

Re-run which processors?

The retry decision
10
Problem 2  /  What we need

Retry is per-processor, not per-request.

Processor Re-run? Why
Semantic cache no Result unchanged in window
Guardrails no Payload unchanged
Semantic routing no Prompt unchanged, same model
Endpoint picker yes Endpoint failed / KV state shifted
Credential injection maybe If token expired
Not in any gateway today
  • Traditional proxy: retry = reconnect & resend the whole request.
  • AI gateway: retry = re-run some processors, skip others — each with its own semantics.
  • If you’ve used Envoy ext_proc you’ve felt this: on retry, does the callout happen again? “It depends.” There’s no way to declare what you want.
Motivates: per-processor retry policy
11
Problem 3 of 3

Policy
scope.

Too broad, too narrow, never right.
Policy attaches at the wrong scope today
12
Problem 3  /  The goldilocks problem

Attach credentials where?

Too broad
At the Gateway
One credential-injection policy, attached to the gateway itself.
Policy applies to routes you never wanted it on.
Too narrow
Per-Route
Copy-paste the same credential policy onto every relevant route.
Duplication. Drift. Rotation churn.
Just right
At the Backend
One policy, attached to the destination — api.openai.com.
Default at the destination. Routes can still override or add.
Gateway · Route · Backend
13
Problem 3  /  Same problem, TLS-shaped

The primitives were built for producers, not consumers.

Today’s egress workaround stacks an ExternalName Service plus a BackendTLSPolicy. Both were designed for the producer — “how clients should connect to me.”
For egress you’re the consumer. You’re saying “this is how I want to connect to you.” The existing surface can’t express that cleanly.
Fragmentation signal
Istio shipped ServiceEntry. Linkerd shipped EgressNetwork. Multiple vendors solved this themselves — a flag that the standard is missing a primitive.
Consumer vs. producer semantics
14
Problem 3  /  The primitive we need

The Backend resource.

A first-class representation of an external destination — with inline, consumer-oriented TLS and policy attachment.
apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1alpha1 kind: Backend metadata: name: openai spec: destination: type: Fqdn fqdn: hostname: api.openai.com ports: - number: 443 protocol: HTTP2 tls: # consumer-side mode: Simple extensions: - name: inject-credentials # policy attaches here config: secretRef: name: openai-api-key
  • Destination as a first-class noun. No synthetic Service pretending to be the upstream.
  • Inline TLS, consumer-side. Host and SNI always agree — both come from the same spec.
  • Policy attachment point. Credentials, rate defaults, circuit breakers, retry defaults.
  • Many routes, one backend. HTTPRoutes targeting it inherit these defaults; routes can override.
  • Internal & external. Same surface for in-cluster pools and cloud fallback.
Motivates: Backend resource + egress gateway
15
02  /  How we’re solving it

Three problems. Three primitives.

Problem 1
Unordered filters, no data flow
PayloadProcessing
Pipeline
Ordered execution. Per-processor timeouts, failure modes, retry semantics. Concurrent where safe.
Problem 2
Retry is all-or-nothing
Per-processor
retry policy
Declare which processors re-run on failure. Idempotent-with-unchanged-input processors skip; state-dependent ones re-run.
Problem 3
No destination-level scope
Backend
+ egress gateway
Consumer-side TLS inline. Policy attaches at the destination. Works for in-cluster pools and external FQDNs.
Primitives, mapped
16
02  /  Primitive detail

PayloadProcessingPipeline.

kind: PayloadProcessingPipeline metadata: name: inference-pipeline spec: processors: - name: semantic-routing backendRefs: [{ name: router-svc }] config: timeout: "100ms" failureMode: closed - name: guardrails backendRefs: [{ name: guardrail-svc }] config: timeout: "500ms" failureMode: closed - name: semantic-cache backendRefs: [{ name: cache-svc }] config: timeout: "200ms" failureMode: open
  • Ordered execution with explicit sequence.
  • Per-processor failure modes — fail-open or fail-closed, per step.
  • Per-processor timeouts and retry semantics.
  • Concurrent execution permitted for validate-only processors.
  • Bidirectional — processes requests and responses.
  • Can short-circuit — cache hit returns immediately; router can redirect.
Primitive #1
17
02  /  Not finished — proposals

Real design questions — open right now.

Wire protocol
gRPC for processor ↔ gateway? What messages? How do chunks stream?
Processing loops
If a mutating processor changes the payload, can it trigger re-evaluation of route matching?
Gateway vs. route scope
When both gateway-level and route-level processors exist, how do they interleave?
Backend × multi-cluster
How does Backend compose with multi-cluster and agentic networking proposals?
Retry API surface
What does “re-run this processor but not that one” actually look like in the YAML? We haven’t agreed yet.
These are proposals, not finished specs
18
02  /  Putting it back together

What an AI Gateway actually does.

Three categories of work.
01
Allow or deny.
Pass the request through, or reject it. No mutation.
guardrails · AuthN / AuthZ · token rate limits
02
Mutate.
Rewrite the request before it reaches the upstream.
semantic routing · credential injection
03
Respond.
Short-circuit the pipeline and return a response directly.
semantic cache
Observe & meter
Token-level cost attribution across workloads — spans all three.
Three categories of work
19
03  /  Close

Why we need an AI Gateway.

01
The body is where the signal is.
The gateway must parse and understand AI protocols — inference requests, A2A, MCP — not just headers.
02
Processing has data dependencies.
Ordered pipelines, not unordered filter bags.
03
Retry is per-processor.
Not per-request. Some re-run, most don’t.
04
Policy needs the right scope.
Per-destination, not just per-route or per-gateway.
05
Egress needs consumer semantics.
Not producer primitives repurposed.
Recap
20
03  /  Call to action

Get involved.

These are proposals, not finished specs. The design questions I showed you are live — your input shapes what ships.
Repo github.com/kubernetes-sigs/wg-ai-gateway
Slack #wg-ai-gateway  (Kubernetes)
Meetings Thursdays · 2 pm EST · weekly
Open for input Payload processing · Retry semantics · Backend × multi-cluster
Scan → WG AI Gateway repo
Community input shapes the spec
21
Fin.
The body
is where
the signal is.
Morgan Foster  ·  @usize  ·  usize.github.io/blog  ·  Co-Chair, WG AI Gateway
Thank you for watching.
